name: Theory of Computation
description: Automata theory, formal languages, computability, computational complexity, algorithmic analysis. The mathematics of what machines can and cannot do, how fast they can do it, and the fundamental limits of mechanized reasoning. This is the ceiling of AI capability — no machine can transcend the Turing limit. Depends on 444 (algebra) and 650 (discrete math via automata and formal languages). Feeds into 699 (optimization via complexity-constrained algorithms) and 700 (numerical methods via algorithmic efficiency).
axioms: Church-Turing thesis: effectively calculable functions = Turing-computable functions
P vs NP: the central open problem — can every efficiently verifiable problem be efficiently solved?
Halting problem is undecidable (Turing 1936): there is no general algorithm to determine if an arbitrary program halts
Gödel's incompleteness theorems: consistent formal systems cannot prove their own consistency, and there exist true but unprovable statements
Cook-Levin theorem: SAT is NP-complete — every NP problem reduces to SAT
Time hierarchy theorem: more time allows more problems to be solved
Rice's theorem: all non-trivial semantic properties of programs are undecidable
reasoning_patterns: complexity class analysis (P, NP, PSPACE, EXPTIME, BPP)
algorithm design and asymptotic analysis (Big-O, Ω, Θ)
undecidability detection via reduction from the halting problem
Gödelian boundary identification: what a system cannot know about itself
formal language classification (regular, context-free, context-sensitive, recursively enumerable)
amortized analysis for data structure performance
boundary_conditions: Cannot solve the halting problem (no exceptions)
Cannot prove system completeness or consistency from within (Gödel)
NP-complete problems require exponential time if P ≠ NP (widely believed)
AI systems cannot exceed the Turing limit — they remain in the same computability class as Turing machines
Some problems are decidable but require astronomically large time/memory
